coredata.dc:title: Recent progress in emerging BiPO4-based photocatalysts: Synthesis, properties, modification strategies, and photocatalytic applications

coredata.dc:description: In this perspective, we have highlighted the current literature and explained the synthesis, structure, morphology, modification strategies, and photocatalytic applications of emerging BiPO4-based photocatalysts. Since BiPO4 is a large bandgap photocatalyst, it uses UV light for the excitation of electrons, and also, the recombination of charge carriers is an issue in BiPO4. Various novel modification strategies of BiPO4 photocatalysts viz. defect modifications, heterojunction formation, phase-junctions, surface plasmon resonance, Schottky junction have been successfully proposed and highlighted. These modifications enhance the light absorption and inhibit the recombination of charge carriers BiPO4 photocatalyst. Finally, future aspects for further research on BiPO4-based photocatalysts are also explored. It expects that BiPO4-based photocatalysts represent a promising strategy for developing practical photocatalysts for energy and environmental remediation applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this perspective, we have highlighted the current literature and explained the synthesis, structure, morphology, modification strategies, and photocatalytic applications of emerging BiPO4-based photocatalysts. Since BiPO4 is a large bandgap photocatalyst, it uses UV light for the excitation of electrons, and also, the recombination of charge carriers is an issue in BiPO4. Various novel modification strategies of BiPO4 photocatalysts viz. defect modifications, heterojunction formation, phase-junctions, surface plasmon resonance, Schottky junction have been successfully proposed and highlighted. These modifications enhance the light absorption and inhibit the recombination of charge carriers BiPO4 photocatalyst. Finally, future aspects for further research on BiPO4-based photocatalysts are also explored. It expects that BiPO4-based photocatalysts represent a promising strategy for developing practical photocatalysts for energy and environmental remediation applications.

ce:section.ce:para:
For the last few decades, environmental pollution and management are among the most significant concerns and challenges against humans [1,2]. Renewable source depletion and environmental pollution will restrict social development and human survival. The growing pollution cast a substantial impact on drinking water quality as most of our drinking water resources are contaminated [3,4]. Industrial waste is one of the most significant contributors to this pollution. Various physical, chemical, and biological techniques are used to treat this contaminated water. Still, most of them either use high energy or give rise to secondary pollutants in water or do incomplete mineralization. AOP's can be a better alternative for wastewater treatment as they can use highly oxidative species (•OH, •O2 −, H2O2) to suppress pollutants [5–7]. The pictorial representation of different classes of AOP's is shown in Fig. 1
[5]. Non-photochemical AOP's are low efficient due to their high energy consumption. Photochemical AOP's are considered the most promising technique for water waste treatment because they use solar energy as a light source for pollutant degradation. Out of all, photocatalysis can be an excellent tool to remove pollutants from contaminated water, and it received significant attention over the last few years [8–11]. Photocatalysis uses less energy, and it can also convert the pollutants into CO2 and H2O, thus, forming no secondary pollutants [12–15].

In photocatalysis, a semiconductor material is used as a photocatalyst in which electrons and hole excitation occur due to light irradiation [3,16,17]. As the light of suitable frequency falls on the semiconductor, electrons excite the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of that semiconductor, leaving behind holes. These excited electrons and holes have the tendency to interact with H2O and dissolved oxygen to form highly oxidation species like •OH, •O2 −, H2O2. These high oxidation species interact with pollutants present in water and degrade them. The schematic illustrations of photocatalysis are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The photocatalytic mechanism mainly depends upon the wavelength of light irradiated on the photocatalyst [17–20]. Since the solar spectrum contains ∼45% visible light, the photocatalysts are more efficient. However, visible light active photocatalysts take much time to degrade the same concentration of pollutants compared with ultra-violet light (UV) active photocatalysts. For example, visible light active BiWO6 photocatalyst can degrade only 25% of methylene blue (MB) (10−5 mol/L) in 2 h, whereas, UV light active TiO2 (100 mg) showed complete degradation of MB (50 mL of 0.1 mM MB) under UV light illumination [21,22]. Also, visible light active photocatalysts have weak redox potential, lowering their ability to mineralize the pollutants into CO2 and H2O [3]. Thus, practical applications of visible light-responsive photocatalyst are still limited. For environmental applications, UV light active photocatalysts are quite meaningful.

Moreover, photocatalytic devices also play an essential role in the overall photocatalytic activity of photocatalyst [23–25]. A photocatalytic device should be made in such a way that it should utilize the maximum light irradiation, not from other external biases. Also, a photocatalytic device should ensure complete light penetration from the source, and the light source should consume low energy. The device should also be air-tight so that the gasses byproducts can be detected easily if required. The material used in semiconductor devices should be inert to photocatalytic reaction so that the semiconductor should not compete with any other light observer in the device. The material used for the construction of the device should be hard enough to ensure safety [26]. Dai et al. constructed a UV-LED/TiO2 photocatalytic device to degrade methylene blue dye, and studied various conditions such as catalyst mass, light power, and pH [27]. Dariani et al. also developed a UV/TiO2 photocatalytic device for the degradation methylene blue which showed 90% degradation efficiency within one hour [28]. Varying radiations and geometry parameters in photocatalytic device can improve the photocatalytic activity. Overall, the properties of photocatalyst semiconductor such as band gap, charge carrier effective masses, binding energy of exitons, dielectric constant etc. strongly influence the efficiency of photocatalytic device. Thus, we required a photocatalyst with high photocatalytic activity, low cost, non-toxicity, and high stability.

Bismuth phosphate - BiPO4 (BPO) is one of the promising UV light active photocatalysts, which showed up to 1–2 times high photocatalytic activity than conventional UV active TiO2 photocatalyst [29–31]. BPO is a photocatalyst with high photocatalytic activity, low cost, and non-toxicity. With some exceptional electronic and optical properties, BPO showed strong mineralization ability against pollutants. BPO showed superior photocatalytic activity under UV light as compared to traditional TiO2 photocatalyst. It also showed enhanced photocatalytic activity in separating radioactive elements, ion sensing, and degradation of pollutants. PO4 3− played an essential role in the improved photocatalytic due to good electron-hole separation.

Moreover, morphology, crystal structure, surface area, and bandgap of BPO are also crucial factors that affect the photocatalytic activity in BPO photocatalyst. Since organic pollutants such as dyes and antibiotics are the significant contributors to water pollution [3,32], synthetic dyes can cause serious health hazards and environmental problems. In contrast, long-term exposure to antibiotics may cause teratogenicity, carcinogenicity, reproductive effects, microbial hazards [33–36]. BPO can be an essential tool for the degradation of various dyes and antibiotics; for example, AgI/BiPO4 composite showed 92.2% efficiency for the degradation of RhB dye after 60 min under visible light irradiation [37]. Other types of dyes such as methylene blue, methyl orange, crystal violet are also degraded by BPO or BPO based photocatalysts [38–43]. BPO also showed 99.9% efficiency for degrading ciprofloxacin antibiotics after 90 min of UV irradiation [44]. Phenol is another pollutant in polluted water, which can be fatal to human beings as it is reported to be a tumor promoter in humans [45,46]. BPO was reported for the complete mineralization of phenol in 4 h [47]. Even more efficient & enhanced activity was shown by BiOCl/BPO photocatalyst for the complete mineralization of phenol due to enhanced charge separation and light-harvesting [48]. Apart from all, benzene is myelotoxic and its chronic exposure may cause blood dyscrasias [49]. Benzene is carcinogenic and hence causes acute myelogenous leukemia. BPO showed a good degradation efficiency against benzene also [50]. In terms of energy production, Pan et al. also explained the photocatalytic property of BiPO4 for H2 production [51]. Pristine BiPO4 was reported to produce 14.9 µ mol/h of H2 in ethanol aqueous solution under UV light irradiation and this production was further enhanced by forming heterojunction of BPO with reduced graphene oxide due to enhanced adsorption, charge separation and transfer. Thus BPO can be an essential and effective tool for water purification via photocatalysis.

Due to the strong redox ability, low cost, and non-toxicity, we have chosen a BPO photocatalyst to review this article. Some articles are already published on Scopus based on BPO, but no one has discussed the BPO photocatalyst and its modifications along with its applications briefly [52,53]. In this review, we will discuss the crystal structure & morphology of BPO and factors affecting the structure of BPO. We will also explore the photocatalytic mechanism of BPO with major drawbacks of pristine BPO photocatalyst. To overcome the limitations of this pure BPO, we will explain the modification to BPO. Finally, we will discuss the applications and future aspects of the BPO photocatalyst. To do this, we have gone through various articles published on Scopus, which are based on BPO. We have searched for articles published from 2010 to 2021 using the keywords “BiPO4 + photocatalysis”. Out of all, we have selected articles written in the English language and contain BPO as a photocatalyst. We have neglected the articles written in a language other than English, and the articles which do not contain BPO based photocatalysts are also neglected. The graphical representation of the selected papers is shown in Fig. 2(b). We will also explore the development issues and future aspects regarding the BPO photocatalyst by reviewing this information.

Hydrothermal synthesis of BPO lead to the formation of three crystalline phases: a hexagonal phase (HBPO) (space group; P3121) and two monoclinic phases (space group; P21/n (nMBPO) & P21/m (mMBPO)) (Fig. 3 (a)) [54,55]. The hexagonal phase formed at lower temperature and acidity, while the monoclinic phases formed at higher temperature and acidity. Symmetry and choice of coordinates are the major differences between the three phases. Otherwise, the crystal structures are almost analogous. In BPO, each Bismuth atom is surrounded by eight oxygen atoms. Four atoms were placed at tetrahedron corners at a distance of 2.33 Å, and the other four are placed at octahedron edges at a distance of 2.66 Å [56]. Each oxygen is bonded to two Bi atoms in the whole structure by one short and one long bond, and each phosphorus atom is coordinated to six Bi atoms. As reported by Pan et al., HBPO and nMBPO exhibited rod-like morphology, whereas mMBPO exhibited plate-like morphology [43]. The SEM images of these different as-synthesized photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 3(b), (c) and (d) [54,57]. Moreover, HBPO, mMBPO, and nMBPO had the bandgap of 4.6, 3.8, and 4.2 eV, respectively. Out of the three, HBPO had the largest BET surface area of 0.82 m2/g, then nMBPO (0.03 m2/g) and mMBPO (0.06 m2/g). However, the photocatalytic activity of BPO depends upon the PO4 3− which would change the distribution of electronic cloud around P-O due to the dipole moment derived from distorted PO4 tetrahedron and hence enhance the electron-hole separation. mMBPO had the most distorted PO4 tetrahedron and hence the highest photocatalytic activity than the other two phases.

DFT studies can be used to determine the density of states and band structure of the BiPO4 photocatalyst (Fig. 4 ) [58]. BPO has a bandgap of 3.5–4.6 eV with valance band maxima (VBM) at 3.08 V and conduction band minima (CBM) at -0.77 V (by electrochemical method) [29,54]. The higher VBM of BPO compared to TiO2 (∼2.9 V), also showed high oxidation ability and thus exhibits high mineralization ability against pollutants [59]. VB of BPO consists of O 2p orbital and some contribution of Bi 6p orbitals, and CB of BPO consists of Bi 6p orbitals with little contribution of O 2p orbitals [60]. The transition from the VB to CB in BPO was observed to be an indirect transition. When a photon of light falls on BPO, the electron excites from HOMO (O 2p) to LUMO (Bi 6p), forming a large electron cloud on PO4 3- group. This electron cloud on PO4 3- repel electrons and attract holes, enhancing the charge carrier's separation [54]. When the electrostatic effect of one group affects the electron density of the other group, this is termed an inductive effect, and this phenomenon is common in phosphate salts [61]. Thus, BPO can be a better photocatalyst for extended complete mineralization of pollutants and enhanced photocatalytic activities. Wang et al. also reported the synthesis of different crystal phases of BPO (hexagonal (HBPO), monoclinic (MBPO) and mixture of both (H/M-BPO)) using different ratios of glycerol: water, reaction temperatures and time [57]. HBPO was synthesized at 160 ⁰C for 1 h using 2:1 ratio of glycerol & water. Whereas, MBPO and H/M-BPO were prepared at 200 ⁰C for 3 h using glycerol and water ratio of 1:2 and 2:1, respectively. Here, HBPO and H/M-BPO showed rice like morphology, whereas, MBPO exhibited rod like morphology. The SEM images of these different as synthesized materials are shown in Fig. 3(e), (f) and (g). Here also, MBPO showed the best photocatalytic activity with high rate of mineralization among three due to intrinsic distortion of PO4 tetrahedron.

BPO with different morphologies and sizes were synthesized via simple methods by controlling the factors like temperature, pH, reaction time, solvents type [53]. As reported, methods such as the hydrothermal method [29,58,62], solvothermal method [44,63], microwave-assisted hydrothermal method [64], ball milling method [65], two-steps solvothermal method [38], electrospinning method [66], were used to synthesize the BPO with desired morphology and surface area. Different dimensional morphologies such as 1D, 2D, and 3D were synthesized using different methods. Generally, 1D BPO comprises of nanorods [65], nano-fibers [66], nanowires [67], nano-needles [39], whereas, 2D BPO includes nanosheets [68] and nanoplates [69]. Main examples of 3D BPO are flower-like spheres [64], urchin-like [70], nano-cocoons [63], dendrite like [71], nano-cubes [62]. Every synthesis route has significance for achieving desired morphology; for example, ultrasonic radiations are quite helpful in the fabrication of 1D and 2D structures of BPO. Therefore, Nithya et al. synthesized hexagonal phase BPO nanorods at room temperature using sonochemical method [72]. Also, BPO nanowires and Nano-cocoons were synthesized at 200 ⁰C using mixed solvents (water/glycerol) [63]. Moreover, nano-cubes, Urchin like nanorods and dendrite like structures were prepared by using hydrothermal method [70]. Furthermore, electrospinning and calcination method was used for the preparation of BPO nanofibers [66]. Different reported morphologies of BPO are shown in Fig. 5 .

Different morphologies have a significant effect on the photocatalytic activity of BPO, as Cheng et al. studied the photocatalytic activity of BPO with different rod-like morphologies prepared with different methods [73]. Hexagonal phase BP nanorods were prepared using a sonochemical process, whereas monoclinic phase nanorods were prepared by the hydrothermal method. Under the sonochemical method, short rod-like structures were formed with a diameter of ∼41.3 nm and length ∼123 nm. However, as the hydrothermal condition was applied, thin rod-like structures with a diameter ∼94 nm and length ∼1430 nm were formed. Furthermore, the sonochemical method followed by the hydrothermal method leads to the formation of thick rod-like structures having a diameter ∼155 nm and length ∼769 nm. Out of all, nanorods prepared with the hydrothermal method showed the maximum photocatalytic activity against methylene blue dye. Low recombination of charge carriers was the main reason for the higher photocatalytic activity than the others. The comparison of photocatalytic activity of different morphologies of BPO is shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting the morphology and structure of BPO
ce:section.ce:para: The formation mechanism of inorganic compounds is always affected by the reaction conditions [68]. Therefore, the formation of BPO was studied under the different conditions of temperature, pH, reaction time, solvent. According to Mooney-Slater, the transition between the hexagonal to monazite phases was not reversible, and no spontaneous change was detected between the two at ordinary temperatures [56]. To understand the relationship between phase structure and photocatalytic activity, fabrication conditions allied to the formation of BPO must be more transparent.

For the synthesis of BPO polymorphs, pH is a determinant parameter. Started from HBPO precursor at 0 pH, pure nMBPO phase was formed, and on increasing the pH from 0 to 1, a mixture of nMBPO and mMBPO phases formed [68]. Further, pure mMBPO was developed when pH increased to 3 and 7. Again, a mix of nMBPO and mNBPO was formed when pH increased to 10. Finally, p-doped bismuth oxide was formed when pH reached 14. Nithya et al. also studied the effect of pH on the BPO crystal using the sonochemical method [74]. At pH 1 to 7, the particles transformed from irregular coarse-shaped structure to rod-like structure, and at pH 9 to 12, it finally changed to irregular aggregate. Thus, the change in pH directly modulates the growth of crystal and kinetics of nucleation. The growth of the crystal in a specific shape depends upon the surface free energy of crystal facet i.e. the growth will be higher if the surface energy is higher. At higher and lower pH, H+ and OH− ions absorbed at the crystal facet and hence hindered the growth of crystal in that direction.

Temperature also has a significant effect on the structure formation of BPO, which is clarified by Pan et al. At lower temperatures, HBPO was formed, whereas an increase in temperature favors the formation of nMBPO and mMBPO [54]. BPO crystal obtained in 12 M H3PO4 for 72 h at 20 °C was HBPO. Similarly, at 40 °C, a mixture of HBPO & nMBPO was formed. Furthermore, at 100 °C and 160 °C, nMBPO and mixture of nMBPO & mMBPO was formed. Finally, mMBPO was formed at 200 °C. With an increase in reaction temperature, the phase transformation accelerated [68]. Zu et al. explained that the increase in calcination temperature up to 400 °C could increase the photocatalytic of BPO [65]. However, further increases in calcination temperature induce aggregation and decrease the photocatalytic activity. Also, higher hydrothermal temperature resulted in the formation of larger particles [31].

The effect of reaction time was also explained by Wang et al. on BMO crystal using HBPO precursor [54]. At pH 0 and temperature 240 ⁰C, a fraction of HBPO converted into nMBPO after 1 h. Whereas complete transformation of HBPO into nMBPO has occurred after 2 h of reaction time. Similarly, at pH 3, the HBPO precursor remained in the mixture after 1 h of reaction time. In contrast, HBPO completely disappeared after 2 h of reaction time, and a combination of nMBPO & mMBPO was formed. Xu et al. also synthesized BPO via the hydrothermal method and studied the effect of reaction time (1, 4, 12, 24, 48, 72 and 96 h) on the formation of BPO [31]. At lower temperatures and reaction times, BPO nanorods were formed. However, when the reaction time increased (48 h), rods were converted into a sheet-like structure with a straight cutting edge, i.e., the hexagonal phase BPO nanorods converted into monoclinic BPO sheets. Also, the perfect BPO sheets were formed as the time increased to72 h. Moreover, this sheet-like structure started to disintegrate as the reaction time increased to 96 h. Out of all, BPO sheets prepared at 180 ⁰C for 72 h showed the highest photocatalytic activity for the degradation of Carbamazepine under UV irradiation due to its monoclinic phase and sheet-like shape. BPO prepared via mixed solvothermal method also showed the change in morphology after different reaction times [63]. BPO synthesized at 200 ⁰C for 1 h showed nano cocoons-like morphology, typically 100 nm to 150 nm. When the reaction time increased to 3 h, these nano cocoons converted to columns like nanorods. Thus, reaction time exhibits a significant impact on the structure formation of BPO, as discussed above.

The use of solvent can also affect the BPO synthesis in morphology and crystal structure. Reactivity, diffusion rate, and solubility of reagents & intermediates can be influenced by solvent. For example, Xue et al. studied the effect of solvent (glycerol and water) on the synthesis of BPO [63]. BPO column-like nanorods were synthesized by using a mixed solvothermal method which was the function of reaction time. Nevertheless, the ratio between 1:1 and 2:1 (glycerol:water) was ideal for forming monoclinic BPO nanorods. When the ratio was above 2:1 or lower than 1:1, irregular nanoparticles and nanorods were formed. Glycerol played a vital role in morphology control as the high viscosity of glycerol altered the diffusion rate of ions in the solution. A too high volume ratio of glycerol to water (5:1) led to the formation of irregular nanoparticles due to the low diffusion of ions. Whereas low glycerol concentration to water (1:2) promoted a high diffusion rate of ions, a rod-like structure was formed.

Thus, the factors mentioned above are very responsible for the morphology and crystal structure control of BPO. These factors can be controlled together to get the appropriate structure and result. For example, Wang et al. discussed the effect of solvent, reaction time, and temperature to form BPO with different crystal structures and morphology as discussed above [57]. Surprisingly, Zhao et al. examined the crystal phase transition between nMBPO and mMBPO simply by hand grinding [75]. When mMBPO was ground for 2 min, nMBPO was formed, but this nMBPO became more dominant after 10 min of grinding. Further increase in the grinding time up to 60 min led to the formation of mMBPO and is converted to pure nMBPO after 90 min of grinding. These are some of the crucial factors which affect the formation, stability, morphology & photocatalytic activity of BPO.

Photocatalysis is considered to be an environmentally friendly technique to empower the green economy. Concerning this, BPO, an oxyacid salt photocatalyst, has been investigated by many researchers since the last decade due to its good optical and electronic properties [31,71,74]. BPO showed an excellent photocatalytic activity under UV light against various pollutants present in water. For example, BPO nanofibers prepared through a simple electrospinning method followed by calcination treatment showed 3.72 times more photocatalytic activity than P25 against alkaline peroxide mechanical pulping (APMP) effluent (0.08 g of photocatalyst in 60 mL APMP effluent) [66]. Almost 88% of APMP effluent was photodegraded by BPO nanofibers under UV light for 120 min, whereas P25 photodegraded only 41% of APMP effluent under the same conditions. Furthermore, only 3% of APMP effluent was photodegraded after 120 min of UV irradiation without any photocatalyst.

Zhang et al. also studied the photocatalytic activity related to different facets of BPO [71]. BPO cubes, six branches, and flower-like dendrites were prepared. Their photocatalytic activities were compared by degradation of Methylene Blue (MB) under UV light (100 mg of photocatalyst in 8 mg/L of MB). Six branch dendrites showed the highest photocatalytic activity of 72.8% compared to the cubes (35.1%) and flower-like dendrites (45.9%). This increased activity was due to the high percentage of (002) facet on six branch dendrites, which had high surface energy and, hence, beneficial to enhancing photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic activity of a photocatalyst is strongly influenced by surface state, crystallinity, surface area, enhanced light absorption, charge carrier recombination. In BPO, distorted PO4 tetrahedron is also a critical factor in the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the crystal [54]. Photocatalytic activities of BPO against different pollutants are shown in Table 2 .

Photoexcitation of photocatalyst via photon of light is the main principle behind photocatalysis. Moreover, the adsorption or diffusion of pollutants over the surface sites of photocatalyst is essential for degrading pollutants [76]. Thus the degradation cannot occur without these phenomena. Since BPO is a large bandgap photocatalyst, it requires UV light to excitation an electron from the valance band to the conduction band [52]. When BPO is placed in the UV light, it absorbs light, and electron/hole generation starts. These electrons and holes migrate to the surface of the photocatalyst and react with H2O, OH− and O2 to form highly reactive •OH and •O2 − radicals [73,77]. Generally, hole capturing the OH− and reduction of oxygen with electrons leads to the formation of •OH and •O2 −. However, in BPO, •OH radicals formed at the surface of BPO were mainly generated by reduction of O2, because the standard redox potential of Bi(III)/Bi(V) (1.59 V at 0 pH) in BPO was lower than that of OH−/•OH (1.99 V) [31].

UV light is the necessary condition for the BPO to form •OH radicals and is mainly formed by reducing O2, but both holes and •OH are equally important depending upon degradation [29,58]. For example, holes are the major reactive species for the degradation of phenol by BPO, although •OH is the most potent oxidizing agent after fluorine [47]. This is because •OH tends to add to the unsaturated C-C bonds or abstract H from the C-H bond. However, if a compound does not have unsaturated C-C bonds and abstractable H, it cannot be mineralized by •OH. Similarly, •OH cannot degrade oxalic acid intermediate formed during mineralization of phenol. Therefore, •OH is superior in the conversion of phenol but inferior in the mineralization of phenol [52]. The degradation mechanism of phenol by BPO is shown in Fig. 6 (a).

Generally, the photocatalytic process of BPO follows pseudo-first-order kinetics [29,52,65]. The photocatalytic activity of BPO depends upon PO4 3− ions. The large negative charge of PO4 3−, maintains a large dipole in BPO, which is responsible for the enhanced separation of charge carriers. During the process, the electron excites from O 2p states to Bi 6p states by the action of photons. Meanwhile, holes are attracted towards the PO4 3− ions having large electron cloud overlapping density. On the other hand, electrons are repelled by PO4 3− ions, and hence, electron/hole separation can be enhanced. Also, the strong P-O bond of PO4 3− hindered the formation of oxygen vacancies which act as recombination centers for electrons and holes. Thus, this enhanced separation leads to more reactive species, which help degrade pollutants to CO2 and H2O and other inorganic compounds. The photocatalytic mechanism of pollutants degradation by BPO is shown below [53]:

Due to the exceptional optical properties, BPO enhanced application in ion sensing separation of radioactive elements and photocatalysis [29,78]. BPO showed good photocatalytic activity as compared to the TiO2 photocatalyst. Moreover, BPO has a stable electronic structure, low cost, and nontoxic [44,52]. Due to the absorption in the high-energy UV region, it displayed enhanced and complete mineralization of pollutants. Nevertheless, as far as the practical applications are concerned, BPO has some significant limitations which hindered its overall efficiency as a photocatalyst. The limits are as follow,
•
Since BPO has a large bandgap that requires high energy for electronic excitations, it narrows the light response of photocatalyst [40]. This large gap limits the light absorption of BPO to the UV region, and no visible light absorption occurs in pristine BPO. Solar light is the cheapest energy source, containing just ∼4% of UV light and almost 43% visible light [79]. Thus, in terms of green energy usage, pristine BPO is not that efficient.
•
Pristine BPO shows poor charge carrier separation due to high recombination centers present on it [53].
•
Pristine BPO has low activity sites, which limit its activity for better absorption and pollutant degradation.
•
Low photocurrent and limited migration/transmission of charge carriers is also a major issue in pristine BPO [80].

To overcome the limitation mentioned above and improve the photocatalytic activity of BPO, various modification strategies such as defect modification, heterojunction, phase junction can be employed [80]. Some critical strategies to improve the activity of BPO photocatalyst are discussed below.

Pristine BPO operates on UV lamps due to its wide-ranging bandgap, which increases its operational cost [52]. Modification to this pristine BPO can be more valuable if it can utilize a large portion of visible light. At the same time, it should not decrease the photocatalytic activity under UV light because solar light contains both UV and visible lights[80]. As the various drawbacks of BPO discussed above, separation & transfer of charge carriers and extended light absorption are major approaches for photocatalytic modifications. According to the studies, a junction between the two phases of a semiconductor or a junction between the two different semiconductors (type-II, z-scheme, Schottky junction etc.) can enhance the charge separation [3]. Facets and surface modification can improve the charge separation and transfer and improve the light absorption. Moreover, surface and bulk defects can also enhance the optical properties of the photocatalyst. This study will enhance the synergetic effect of these approaches for the better advancement of BPO photocatalyst.

Facet engineering is a versatile approach for photocatalytic performance enhancement [81]. It notes that the generation, transfer, and consumption of charges in photocatalytic redox reactions are the major steps that determine the overall efficiency of the photocatalyst. Facet engineering may facilitate these steps to enhance photocatalytic activity. Different orientations of facets are responsible for the change in surface coordinate and atomic arrangement. Some of the major advantages of facet engineering are,
•
Adsorption and activation of molecules greatly depend upon a surface atomic arrangement which ultimately enhances the selectivity and activity of photocatalyst.
•
Facet engineering can also tune the redox ability of photocatalyst by modifying surface electronic band structure.
•
As the charge densities, separation, and transfer depend upon crystal orientation; thus, facet engineering can be crucial for improving these key factors.

Zhang et al. in 2014 explored the critical aspect of facets for BiPO4 by using the hydrothermal method of synthesis [71]. Hydrogen ion concentration (i.e., pH) showed a significant effect on the formation of facets. It had a substantial influence on morphology because hydrogen ions can control the release rate of Bi(III). (002) facet of BiPO4 showed the highest surface energy and high content of suspended oxygen atoms which trap electrons to enhance the charge separation. Thus, (002) facet ultimately enhanced the photocatalytic activity of BiPO4. Zheng et al. also explained the photocatalytic activity of different BiPO4 facets [82]. Again, (002) facet showed higher surface energy as compared to the (012), (031) and (200) facets. The high-energy facet contains more unsaturated atoms adsorbing O2, H2O, pollutants, and facilitating redox reaction. Therefore, (002) facet BiPO4 showed 96.6% degradation efficiency and 72% mineralization efficiency against benzene pollutants.

To maneuver the photocatalytic performance, defect engineering has been proved to be an essential parameter [83,84]. Defects are corresponding to the disruption of the periodic arrangement of atoms or molecules in a crystalline material. Defects can exist in almost all photocatalytic materials and significantly affect photocatalytic activity. Various defects such as doping, vacancy defects, skew defects, edge defects, twin defects, grain defects, lattice disorder, and voids are major defects reported in photocatalysts. An illustrative representation of various defects in a photocatalyst lattice is shown in Fig. 6(b). Defects are located at the bulk or on the surface of the photocatalytic material. Generally, doping and vacancies can form both surface & bulk defects. Boundaries and dislocation are examples of bulk defects, but their terminations are exposed on the surface and considered a surface defect. Different defects may co-exist in a photocatalyst, i.e., with different locations and atomic structures.

In addition to surface and bulk defects, a mutual interface is formed due to the face-to-face contact between the two photocatalysts (i.e., heterojunctions, homojunction). Interacial defects are formed in the hybrid structure [83,85]. These defects may be the result of surface defects of photocatalysts in contact. Defects can extend the light absorption capacity, enhanced charge separation, good charge transfer, and improved surface photocatalytic reaction. Besides all advantages, defects can act as recombination centers for charge carriers and change the original crystal's electronic structure, acting as a scattering center for charge carriers. This ultimately hindered the diffusion of charge carriers. Thus a defect-controlled photocatalyst should be developed along with a broad understanding of the mechanism to take full advantage of defects.

Reduction of host nanocrystal often introduces vacancies into the lattice of photocatalyst [83,86,87]. For example, oxygen vacancies are formed in the lattice of metal oxide when the oxygen atoms are removed from the lattice by treating it as a reductant such as NaBH4. The interaction of H from the reductant with lattice oxygen leads to the formation of oxygen vacancies. Along with the chemical reduction method, sometimes photo-reduction methods are also used for creating oxygen vacancies in some photocatalysts [52,88]. Apart from the reduction method, vacancies can also be created by thermal treatment. High temperature promotes band cleavage, which leads to the escape of atoms from the lattice of the photocatalyst. An oxygen-deficient environment is required during the thermal treatment to produce oxygen vacancies in the lattice by thermal treatment. Similarly, other vacancies can be produced by adding a suitable etching agent that removes atoms selectively.

BPO with oxygen vacancies was first fabricated by facile soft chemical approach, vacuum deoxidation [89]. These vacancies enhanced the photocurrent and photocatalytic activity BPO to 2.5 and 1.5 times, respectively. Since the bandgap of pure BPO is ∼4.1 eV, i.e., it will absorb light at ∼300 nm, and no absorption of light should be there at 365 nm. Nevertheless, due to an increase in shallow states, the light response of BPO with oxygen vacancies was enhanced, and an excellent photocatalytic activity was absorbed in 365 nm. The oxygen vacancies states formed just above the valance band and partly overlap with the valance band of BPO (Fig. 6(c) and (d)) [90]. This broadens the width of the valance band of BPO, which intern narrows the bandgap and enhances the visible light response. BPO prepared with solvothermal calcination method also shows oxygen vacancy defects, which showed enhanced photocatalytic activity compared to other BPO photocatalyst prepared without calcination (i.e. without oxygen vacancies) [91]. Oxygen vacancies on the photocatalyst surface can be confirmed by in-situ electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR). The comparison of ESR spectra of BPO prepared at 140 ⁰C by solvothermal method (S-BPO), and BPO prepared after calcination at 140 ⁰C (C-BPO), is shown in Fig. 6(e). This spectrum indicates higher surface vacancies in C-BPO, due to which C-BPO showed three times higher photocurrent than S-BPO. This high photocurrent ensures quick migration and enhances the separation of charge carriers. Good charge separation efficiency was confirmed using an electron impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plot in which a small arc radius indicates high charge separation. C-BPO showed a smaller arc radius on the Nyquist plot than S-BPO, which confirmed the enhanced charge separation due to the surface oxygen vacancies. BPO with the oxygen vacancies also showed good stability as its photocatalytic activity was slightly even used after 10 months [90].

When the concentration of oxygen vacancies is low, there was a partial overlap of oxygen vacancies states with the valance band of BPO, which improves the photocatalytic activity. However, when the concentration of oxygen vacancies increased, there was an increase in the oxygen vacancy states in the deep forbidden gap, which acted as a recombination center and decreased photocatalytic activity. Also, oxygen vacancies at the surface and no vacancies at the bulk can enhance the photocurrent and ultimately increase the photocatalytic activity.

In doping, impurities are intentionally added to control the properties of bulk photocatalyst [83,92–94]. Doping introduces the defect states in the band gap of photocatalyst, which retard the recombination of charge carriers, alter band gap and enhance light absorption. Generally, photocatalyst are doped with metal ions (noble metals and transition metals) and non-metals [95] [3]. Selective dopants are preferred to achieve the desired goals of altered band gap and good charge transfer. Generally, metal doping forms intraband states near the CB, leading to a red shift of absorption wavelength, i.e., lower the conduction band minima. To control the recombination of charge carriers and upshift the top of valance band, non-metal dopants are widely used. The crystal structure of the semiconductor gets distorted due to doping, and trap sites are formed, which capture the charge carriers and inhibit the recombination [96]. Moreover, double dopants (co-doping) can also enhance solubility and improve the stability of dopants [94].

For example, BPO doped with carbon elements showed 2.6 times higher photocatalytic activity than the bulk BPO [44,97]. Carbon doping upshifts the valance band of BPO from 2.66 to 2.34 by introducing intraband states and improve the light absorption, which ultimately enhances the photocatalytic activity. The schematic illustration of the comparison of bulk BPO with carbon-doped BPO is shown in Fig. 6(f). Li et al. synthesized fluorine-doped BPO via the in-situ fluorination method [40]. Almost 30% enhanced photocatalytic activity was reported at a 0.03 molar ratio of F/Bi. Fluorine replaced the oxygen atoms of BPO, which elevate polarizability and induced dipole moment. Increased dipole moment can intern increase electron-hole separation. Fluorine doping also shows a slight red shift in light absorption.

Nithya et al. synthesized samarium (Sm) metal-doped BPO, which showed 97% degradation efficiency after 50 min of UV light irradiation against methylene blue, which was much better than pure BPO (66%) [41]. Sm doped BPO showed improved photocatalytic activity because electrons may get trapped in partially filled f-orbital of Sm3+, hindering recombination of charge carriers. Also, the unstable Sm2+ 6f states can quickly transfer electrons to O2, which form •O2 − and ultimately create •OH. Similarly, Yb3+ doped BMO also influences the absorption edge of BPO to enhance photocatalytic activity [98]. Yb3+ doping inhibits the electron-hole recombination as the charge carriers get trapped by the Yb-4f level. Moreover, BPO is also co-doped with Tb+ & Eu3+, which showed enhanced photocatalytic activity against Rhodamine B dye under UV light compared to pure BPO [78]. This enhanced photocatalytic activity was due to the synergetic effect of Eu3+ and Tb3+ dopants in the crystal, enhancing the activity by trapping the charge carriers and improving separation.

Although doping is an essential tool for photocatalytic activity enhancement, access doping may cause carrier recombination and thermal instability [32]. Thus an adequate amount of doping is required to acquire the full benefits of doping in a photocatalyst.

Recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, slow migration of charges, and poor light absorption are major issues against a photocatalyst [99]. The separation of charge carriers can be a crucial point to enhance the photocatalytic activity of a photocatalyst. Charge carrier's separation can be enhanced by,
•
Changing morphology increases the surface area and shorten the charge diffusion pathway
•
Removing either electrons or holes from the system by using scavengers (sacrificial solution)
•
Creating heterojunction in which electrons transferred to one semiconductor and holes to the other ensure an enhanced life-time of charge carriers

The heterojunction is an interface between the photocatalysts with different band structures [3,99]. Two semiconductors can be n-type or p-type, and absolute deep electron densities lead to bending bands near the junction, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). Heterojunctions can significantly enhance the photocatalytic activity of a photocatalyst. For example, Yin et al. synthesized BiPO4–BiOBrxI1-x heterojunction, which showed enhanced photocatalytic activity to reduce carbon dioxide under visible light irradiation compared to pristine BiPO4 and BiOBrxI1-x [100]. Since pure BiPO4 has a band gap of ∼4.2 eV and can not absorb visible light. However, by constructing a heterojunction with BiOBrxI1-x, visible light absorption ability is enhanced in BiPO4–BiOBrxI1-x photocatalyst. Heterojunction also enhanced the charge separation of photogenerated charge carriers and showed improved CO2 reduction to CO & CH4. Out of all different compositions, 5%BiPO4-BiOBr0.75I0.25 showed the highest photocatalytic activity for the degradation of CO2 under visible light irradiation for 4 h. Thus, heterojunction formation is a reliable technique to enhanced charge separation and improve light absorption. Three primary types of heterojunction architecture are type-I (Straddling gap), type-II (staggered gap), and type-III (broken gap) heterojunctions (Fig. 7(b), (c) and (d)) [42]. As shown in the figure (A and B represents the two semiconductors), in type-I heterojunctions, CB of A is lower than B. In contrast, the VB of A is higher than B, which leads to the accumulation of electrons and holes on A. In type-II heterojunctions, A has lower CB and VB edges as compared to B. Electrons will transfer from CB of B to the CB of A, and holes will transfer from the VB of A to the VB of B, which leads to the efficient charge separation. A significant difference in CB and VB positions of A and B gives an effective driving force for carrier transfer in type-III heterojunctions. Out of all, type-II semiconductors are proved to be more efficient for electron-hole separation and better photocatalytic activity.

Depending upon the number of semiconductors joined together, heterojunctions can be classified as binary, ternary, and quaternary heterojunction. As discussed above, type-II heterojunction systems are more efficient and can be better for photocatalytic activity enhancement. The charge transfer mechanism of type-II heterojunction is shown in Fig. 7(e). The heterojunction strategy is quite useful in electron-hole separation and good light utilization. For example, Miasang et al. synthesized BiOBr/BiPO4 composite with a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method and compared the recombination rate of charge carriers in pure BiPO4 & BiOBr/BiPO4 [101]. BiOBr/BiPO4 composite separates the charge carriers efficiently, and this charge migration on BiOBr/BiPO4 photocatalyst is illustrated in Fig. 8 (a). Since photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy is used to determine the recombination rate of charge carriers, higher PL intensity showed high recombination, and low PL intensity showed low recombination of charge carriers. The PL intensities of pure BPO and BiOBr/BiPO4 composite prepared with different mole percentages of BiOBr are shown in Fig. 8(b). Pure BPO showed higher PL intensity, which means high recombination, and BiOBr/BiPO4 composite showed low PL intensity, reflecting relatively low recombination. Also, the recombination rate decreased with an increase in BiOBr loading. Moreover, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) also confirmed good charge separation as EIS arc radius for BiOBr/BiPO4 is smaller as compared to BiPO4, which showed that BiOBr/BiPO4 has low electronic impedance and hence higher charge separation and the EIS arc radius of BiOBr/BiPO4 in light is also smaller as compared to the EIS in the dark. (Fig. 8(c) and (d)). Due to good charge separation, BiOBr/BiPO4 composite showed higher photocatalytic activity than pure BPO. g-C3N4/BiPO4 (GCN/BPO) composite also confirmed the enhancement in charge separation due to the formation of heterojunctions [102]. EIS spectra of GCN/BPO photocatalyst are shown in Fig. 9 (a), which showed a smaller relative EIS arc radius than pure BPO. Geioushy et al. compared the photocatalytic activity of pure BiPO4 with binary BiPO4/Bi2S3 (BPO/BS) for Cr(VI) reduction [103]. In the presence of tartaric acid (hole scavenger), BPO/BS showed 97.9% reduction efficiency of toxic Cr(VI) to harmless Cr(III) within 20 min under visible light irradiation, which is much higher than pure BiPO4 under the same set of conditions. The charge transfer followed the type-II mechanism and its illustrative representation along with Cr(VI) reduction is shown in Fig. 9(d). The enhanced photocatalytic activity was attributed to enhanced charge separation and improved light absorption.

Despite binary heterojunction, ternary and quaternary heterojunction provides an additional edge to the photocatalytic activity [3,32]. For example, BiVO4/BiPO4/GO ternary photocatalyst showed 31.5 times higher photocatalytic activity than pure BPO under visible light irradiation[104]. BiVO4/BiPO4/GO showed 99% degradation of reactive blue dye within 60 min of visible light irradiation and 85% degradation efficiency even after 8 cycles. Similarly, Ag/Ag2O/BiPO4/BiMoO6 quaternary heterojunction showed enhanced photocatalytic activity in which BiPO4 & BiMoO6 improved the charge transfer whereas Ag nanoparticles enhanced the charge separation by trapping charge carriers [105]. Also, Ag0/Ag+ consumed the charge carriers and facilitate oxidation process. Thus, this synergetic effect of different photocatalysts can enhance the photocatalytic activity.

Although type-II heterojunctions are pretty valuable for photo-induced charge separation, they still have some drawbacks, viz reduction and oxidation occur at lower potentials. Thus, the redox ability of the photocatalyst decrease in type-II heterojunction, as shown in Fig. 7(f). Also, the electronic repulsions between the charged particles can cause difficulty for the migration of charge carriers between the two semiconductors [3]. To overcome these limits, the Z-scheme heterojunction technique was employed.

To overcome the drawbacks of type-II heterojunctions and to increase the redox ability of photocatalysts, Z-scheme heterojunctions were employed [106–109]. Z-scheme was firstly introduced by the bard in 1972, in which two semiconductors were connected with a shuttle redox mediator, i.e., an electron acceptor/donor pairs, as shown in Fig. 9(b) [110]. This shuttle redox mediator transfers the electrons from CB of semiconductor B to the VB of semiconductor A, which enhanced the redox ability and charge separation in semiconductors. However, the strategy was only applicable to the liquid phase, and a reverse redox mediator can promote the backward reaction. Thus, Tada et al. introduced the concept of all-solid-state (ASS) Z-scheme in 2006, in which metal nanoparticles were used as electron mediators [111].

Nevertheless, rare and expensive noble metals limit the efficiency of the ASS z-scheme [112]. To enhance the efficiency of the Z-scheme photocatalyst further, Yu et al. in 2013 introduced the concept of direct z-scheme, which is mediator-free [113]. Direct Z-scheme is formed by direct contact of semiconductors without a mediator, which reduces the construction cost to a great extent. Direct Z-scheme is similar to type-II heterojunction, but the charge carrier transfer mechanism is different from type-II heterojunctions. The comparison of charge transfer in type-II and Z-scheme heterojunction is shown in Fig. 7(e) and (f). Direct Z-scheme improves the electron-hole separation, redox ability, and overall photocatalytic activity of photocatalyst.

For example, Bi2WO6/BiPO4 (BWO/BPO) Z-scheme composite prepared by Su et al. using ultrasonic chemical method showed 4.29 times higher photocatalytic activity than pristine BPO [114]. This enhanced photocatalytic activity was due to the excellent charge separation, increased surface area, and improved redox ability of the BWO/BPO photocatalyst. The schematic representation of charge transfers and crystal structure of BWO/BPO is shown in Fig. 9(c). Electrons from the CB of Bi2WO6 combined with holes at the VB of BPO. Also, an external electric field was induced due to the internal polar field of Bi2WO6 & BPO, with the formation of P-O-W bonds at the interface. P-O-W bond acted as a bridge to combine electrons from the WO4 layer and holes at BPO (PO4 tetrahedron). This enhanced the separation, migration rate, and redox potential, which increased the pollutant degradation efficiency.

Moreover, z-scheme BiPO4/BiOCl0.9I0.1 photocatalyst showed superior photocatalytic activity against the degradation of phenol and RhB dye pollutants [115]. The proposed mechanism of this z-scheme is shown in Fig. 10 (a). An internal electric field was generated in this photocatalyst at the interface, directing from BiOCl0.9I0.1 to BPO. Thus, electrons migrated from the CB of BPO to the VB of BiOCl0.9I0.1 and recombined with the holes present in the VB of BiOCl0.9I0.1. Retained electrons in the CB of BiOCl0.9I0.1 and holes in the VB of BPO promoted the redox reaction with good separation and prolonged life-time of charge carriers.

Thus, Z-scheme photocatalysis can enhance charge separation, life-time of charge carriers, increase redox ability and improve the overall efficiency. Recently the concept of s-scheme heterojunctions is also introduced, which is nearly based upon the Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism [32, 116]. In s-scheme heterojunction, two n-type semiconductors (S-I & S-II) are connected to form a junction between them. Due to the induced internal electric field, electrons from the CB of S-I recombine with the holes in the VB of S-II, at the interface. Thus, improved separation of photogenerated electrons (in the CB of S-II) and holes (in the VB of S-I) can be achieved for efficient photocatalysis. S-scheme photocatalysis can enhance the overall redox ability, improve charge separation and increase the photocatalytic efficiency. For example, Bi2MoO6/g-C3N4/Au S-scheme photocatalyst showed 13.1 times higher photocatalytic activity than pristine g-C3N4 and 9.7 times higher photocatalytic activity than pure Bi2MoO6, for the degradation of rhodamine B dye [117].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface plasmon resonance and Schottky heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: In a conducting material, collective oscillations of free charge form plasmon [118,119]. These surface plasmons interact strongly with the light of suitable frequency. Light with a frequency above the plasmon frequency gets transmitted, whereas light with a frequency below the plasmon frequency is reflected due to electrons [120,121]. Light of plasmon frequency gets absorbed, and resonance occurs when the real part of the dielectric function goes to zero. Metal nano particles and the light of plasmon frequency lead to the generation of an intense electric field at the surface of the photocatalyst. Generally, a junction formed between a semiconductor and a noble atom (Au, Ag, etc.) form surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [32,122]. SPR either re-emit the photons or can form non-radiating electrons & holes. Electrons from the noble-metal transfer to the conduction band of semiconductor photocatalyst by SPR.

Nevertheless, enhanced induced electric field due to SPR plays a more critical role in enhancing photocatalytic performance than electron transfer. Electrons from the noble-metal SPR can improve the light scattering, promote charge transfer and enhance the light-harvesting capacity of a photocatalyst. Ag is reported as the most prominent material for SPR due to its low cost, high thermal and electrical conductivity, and it can also enhance the life-time of charge carriers due to its ability to accept photogenerated electrons. For example, SPR enhanced the visible light absorption in BiPO4/Au-Pd composite (BPO/Au-Pd). This BPO/Au-Pd composite showed approximately 25 times higher photocatalytic activity than pristine BPO due to the SPR effect of Au and Pd [118]. Also, the photocatalytic activity of pristine Au-Pd was negligible, proving that photocatalytic activity was enhanced due to the SPR effect of Au-Pd. A possible reaction mechanism along with the charge transfer mechanism in BPO/Au-Pd is shown in Fig. 10(b). Due to the SPR effect of BPO/Au-Pd photocatalyst excite under visible light irradiation. Also, electron transfer from the CB of BPO to the CB of Au-Pd nanoparticles could enhance the separation of charge carriers, leading to improve photocatalytic activity ultimately. Moreover, Bi metal with BiPO4 also exhibits an SPR effect, which showed 32.8% degradation efficiency for NO under visible light illumination [123]. The proposed mechanism for the degradation of NO over BPO photocatalyst is shown in Fig. 10(c). Hot carriers were generated due to the SPR effect of Bi-metal and were transferred to BPO, which lead to the formation of •O2 − and •OH (highly oxidative species). Thus, the SPR effect of metals can significantly enhance the photocatalytic activity of photocatalyst.

Also, SPR leads to random charge distribution due to the uncertain direction of charge diffusion and a lack of driving force to transfer charge carriers to the semiconductor [3,32]. A Schottky junction is developed, which overcomes these limitations, as electron transfer from semiconductor to metal and is restricted to flow back to a semiconductor. Thus, charge density and separation can be enhanced due to this unidirectional transfer of charges in the Schottky junction. The comparison of junctions and mechanism of charge transfer is SPR, and Schottky junction is shown in Fig. 10(d) and (e) [124]. A Schottky junction helps improve the photocatalytic activity and overall efficiency of a photocatalyst. For example, the Schottky junction formed between Ti3C2/BiPO4 (TC-BPO) enhanced the electron-hole separation, visible light response, inhibit photocorrosion and improve photostability of BPO [125]. TC-BPO can degrade up to 100% of RhB in 20 min under visible light irradiation. The comparison of degradation efficiency and reaction kinetics of TC-BPO & BPO is shown in Fig. 11 . Ti 3d in Ti3C2 exhibits strong redox ability, enhancing the concentration of highly reactive species in the reaction mixture. The Schottky junction between TC-BPO enhances the overall photocatalytic activity as it forms ∼6 times & 4.3 times more •OH & •O2 −, respectively, as compared to BPO. The above results showed good photocatalytic activity against phenol & dyes (rhodamine B, Methyl orange, anionic dye, etc.)

Efficient separation of electrons and holes can also be achieved by surface junction formed between two different phases of the same semiconductors [52]. For example, Zhu et al. synthesized nMBPO-mMBPO surface phase junction by calcinating HBPO, which showed 28.7 times higher photocatalytic activity again methylene blue (MB) [126]. The temperature had a significant effect on forming different crystal phases of BPO (HBPO, nMBPO & mMBPO). At 450 ⁰C, all the three phases of BPO existed simultaneously, but as the temperature reached 500 ⁰C, HBPO completely disappeared. The crystal composition of BPO at different temperatures is shown in Fig. 12 (a). mMBPO composition readily increased at 550 ⁰C, and nMBPO completely disappeared at 650 ⁰C. The photocatalytic activity first increased when temperature increased up to 500 ⁰C and decreased further increment in temperature. The rate constants of BPO at different temperatures for the degradation of MB are shown in Fig. 12(b). HBPO-nMBPO junction was formed at 400 ⁰C which showed higher photocatalytic activity compared to HBPO & mMBPO alone. Moreover, nMBPO-mMBPO junction was formed at 500 ⁰C, which showed good photocatalytic activity against phenols and dyes (rhodamine B, Methyl orange, anionic dyes), which was 5 times higher than the mixture of two phases. The charge migration mechanism of these two-phase junctions is shown in Fig. 12(c) and (d), which showed an improved separation of charge carriers via phase junctions.

Thus, BPO can be an essential tool for efficient photocatalysis with enhanced redox ability and can be helpful for green energy in the future. BPO based composites showed a significant improvement in photocatalytic activity, and further improvements are required in this area.

Some other surface modifications such as coupling of BPO with carbon quantum dots (CQD's) [127–129], graphene [130], graphene oxide (GO) [131], reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [132], etc. can also be done to enhance the photocatalytic performance. Coupling with CQD's can modify the photocatalytic activity of narrow and wide band gap photocatalysts. CQD's can enhance the adsorption of pollutants on photocatalyst and can also act as support material. CQD's has sp2 graphitic core surrounded by sp3 carbons. CQD's have an exceptional property of altering the low energy photons into high energy photons and hence possess photoluminescence upconversion property which enhances the light-harvesting property of photocatalyst. Thus, CQD's can modify the wide band gap photocatalysts, which absorb smaller wavelengths and improve their light absorption towards larger wavelengths. Moreover, CQD's can act as an electron sink to reduce recombination and enhance photocatalytic activity. This property of reducing charge carrier recombination of CQD's helps improve the photocatalytic activity of narrow band gap materials as charge carrier recombination is a big issue in narrow band gap materials. Furthermore, the materials like graphene can act as support, adsorbent, photostabalizer, photosensitizer, co-catalyst and photocatalyst. Just like CQD's graphene can also enhance the adsorption of pollutants which improves the photocatalytic activity. It can also act as a surfactant and powerful plasmonic material. Other materials such as GO & rGO can also be used to enhance the adsorption of pollutants and improve photocatalytic activity. Thus, such modifications to the surface of BPO can be quite helpful to enhance the photocatalytic performance of this wide band gap photocatalyst.

For example, Di et al. synthesized nitrogen doped quantum dots/BPO (N-CQD/BPO) material, which showed an increase in photocatalytic activity of BPO on the introduction of N-CQD's for the degradation of pollutants such as antibiotic ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, tetracycline & phenol 4-chlorophenol under UV light irradiation [133]. N-CQD's sustainably activate molecular oxygen for pollutant degradation under UV light. On the introduction of N-CQD's, photoluminescence (PL) intensity decreased compared to pristine BPO, which showed enhanced charge separation in N-CQD's/BPO compared to BPO. This N-CQD's/BPO photocatalyst showed 87.5% of ciprofloxacin degradation under UV light within 120 min which is much higher when compared with pristine BiPO4 (64.4%). Also, Zhang et al. synthesized BPO/rGO photocatalyst in which BPO was immobilized on rGO sheets [134]. Along with immobilization, these rGO sheets enhanced the optical properties of BPO, which improve the light-harvesting property of photocatalyst. BPO/rGO showed higher photocatalytic activity for the degradation of pollutants (methylene blue) than pristine BPO. Since BPO has poor adsorption properties and can adsorb only 5% of methylene blue dye, which leads to just 75% degradation of methylene blue within 60 min under UV light. Whereas rGO enhanced the adsorption properties of BPO/rGO photocatalyst and showed 100% degradation of methylene blue within 45 min of UV irradiation. Similarly, Chen et al. synthesized graphene-like boron nitride modified BPO photocatalyst (BN/BPO), which showed good photocatalytic activity for the degradation of enrofloxacin (91.5%) under UV light irradiation for 2 h [135]. The photocatalyst activity was enhanced due to the high separation of charge carriers on the introduction of graphene-like boron nitride. Therefore, as discussed above, coupling the BPO photocatalyst with some suitable material can enhance its degradation efficiency and overall photocatalytic performance.

Thus, BPO can be an essential tool for efficient photocatalysis with enhanced redox ability and can be helpful for green energy in the future. BPO based composites showed a significant improvement in photocatalytic activity, and further improvements are required in this area. The comparison of photocatalytic activity of different modification engineering strategies for BiPO4 is shown in table 3 .

This review discussed the crystal structure, morphology, photocatalytic activity, modification, and applications of BPO photocatalyst. BPO is a UV light active photocatalyst with high mineralization ability compared to visible light active photocatalysts as visible light active photocatalysts have weak redox forces. BPO with space group P21/n showed excellent photocatalytic activity and mineralization ability compared to other crystal phases of BPO. The dipole moment of distorted PO4 3− tetrahedron is responsible for the enhanced activity of BPO. The large bandgap of BPO, slow migration of charge carriers, and high recombination are some of the drawbacks of BPO, which can be overcome by modifications like defects, heterojunctions, phase junctions. The alterations to BPO can enhance its charge separation, charge migration, and light absorption. BPO has strong mineralization ability along with good stability and non-toxicity. Therefore, this review aims to provide a brief overview of BiPO4 and BiPO4 based photocatalysts for pollutant degradation and environmental application.

The development of BPO photocatalysts is still in infancy and required more action in the future.
(1)
In the majority of studies, small-scale applications of BPO are discussed. Thus a cost-effective and extensive scale application of BPO photocatalysts should be performed.
(2)
More chemical and structural modifications can be done to BPO to form different doped and heterostructured photocatalysts with highly efficient and enhanced photocatalytic activity.
(3)
An in-depth investigation of the mechanism of charge transfer in BPO based composites should be done using various computational techniques such as density functional theory. This may help fabricate more efficient photocatalysts.
(4)
BPO with different morphologies should be developed, and more photocatalytic pollutant degradation studies other than dyes should be investigated.
(5)
Photocatalytic activities other than pollutant degradation such as green energy production, bacterial degradation, water splitting should be further explored to improve the future aspects of BPO.
(6)
C60 can play an essential role in storing and shuttling the photogenerated electrons involved in photocatalytic reactions. Thus, forming a composite based on BiPO4 and C60 can efficiently separate the charge carriers and enhance photocatalytic activity.
(7)
Degradation of pollutants by photocatalyst is mainly ascribed to the adsorption of contaminants on the surface of the catalyst. Thus by improving the surface area and crystallinity of BiPO4 based photocatalysts or connecting with materials having more adsorption ability, improvement in photocatalytic activity can be achieved.
(8)
Since actual polluted water contains multiple ions, pollutants, microorganisms, etc., the application of BiPO4 photocatalysts for actual water treatment is still a big challenge.

This review will be pretty helpful in exploring the properties of BPO and its applications for pollutant degradation. Moreover, it can help understand the challenges against the development of BPO and improve future research based on BPO photocatalysts.
